en.extract:
The Barbados bullfinch (Loxigilla barbadensis) is a seedeater bird that is found only on the Caribbean island-nation of Barbados, where it is the only endemic bird species.


Taxonomy
The Barbados bullfinch was previously considered a subspecies of the Lesser Antillean bullfinch (Loxigilla noctis), which is found on neighboring islands.  Despite the misleading nature of its name, the Barbados bullfinch is not a bullfinch at all but a seedeater.  The bird is known locally as a Sparrow or Sparky.


Description
The Barbados bullfinch is a small bird, 14–15 cm (5.5–6 in). The upperparts are a dark olive-grey, the wings are mostly brown, underparts are greyish, while the under tail-coverts are tawny. The species is not sexually dimorphic, with females and males having similar plumage. 
The birds' calls include simple twittering, an occasional harsh petulant note, and a sharp trill [1].


Distribution and habitat
The Barbados bullfinch is found only on the island of Barbados. The birds' habitat includes shrubbery and forest undergrowth; the species has adapted well to humans, often being found in close proximity to areas of human habitation, such as gardens.


Reproduction
Barbados bullfinches construct a globular nest, with a side entrance, in a tree or shrub. The species lays two to three spotted eggs.


Behavior
Barbados bullfinches are extremely innovative and tame birds. Barbados bullfinches living in urban environments were found to have better problem-solving skills and a better immunocompetence than the ones living in rural areas of Barbados. As for their temperament, urban Barbados bullfinches were found to be bolder but more neophobic than their rural counterparts.


Gallery


References

"Barbados Bullfinch (Loxigilla barbadensis) - BirdLife species factsheet". birdlife.org. 2012. Retrieved 26 January 2012.


External links

The '''Barbados bullfinch''' ('''''Loxigilla barbadensis''''') is a [[seedeater]] bird that is found only on the [[Caribbean]] island-nation of [[Barbados]], where it is the only [[endemism|endemic]] bird species.

== Taxonomy ==
The Barbados bullfinch was previously considered a [[subspecies]] of the [[Lesser Antillean bullfinch]] (''Loxigilla noctis''), which is found on neighboring islands.<ref>{{ cite journal | last1=Buckley | first1=P.A. | last2=Buckley | first2=F.G. | year=2004 | title=Rapid speciation by a Lesser Antillean endemic, Barbados Bullfinch ''Loxigilla barbadensis'' | journal=Bulletin of the British Ornithologists' Club | volume=125 | pages=108–123 | url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/40056085 }}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal | last1=Banks | first1=R.C. | last2=Cicero | first2=C. | last3=Dunn | first3=J.L. | last4=Kratter | first4=A.W. | last5=Rasmussen | first5=P.C. | last6=Remsen | first6=J.V. | last7=Rising | first7=J.D. | last8=Stotz | first8=D.F. | date=2006 | title=Forty-Seventh Supplement to the American Ornithologists' Union Check-List of North American Birds | journal=The Auk | volume=123 | issue=3 | pages=926–936 | doi=10.1093/auk/123.3.926 | doi-access=free}}</ref>  Despite the misleading nature of its name, the Barbados bullfinch is not a [[Pyrrhula|bullfinch]] at all but a [[seedeater]].  The bird is known locally as a Sparrow or Sparky.

== Description ==
The Barbados bullfinch is a small bird, 14–15 cm (5.5–6 in). The upperparts are a dark olive-grey, the wings are mostly brown, underparts are greyish, while the under tail-coverts are tawny. The species is not [[sexually dimorphic]], with females and males having similar [[plumage]]. 
The birds' calls include simple twittering, an occasional harsh petulant note, and a sharp trill [http://www.xeno-canto.org/species/Loxigilla-barbadensis].

== Distribution and habitat ==
The Barbados bullfinch is found only on the island of [[Barbados]]. The birds' [[habitat]] includes shrubbery and [[forest]] [[undergrowth]]; the species has adapted well to [[humans]], often being found in close proximity to areas of human habitation, such as [[gardens]].

== Reproduction ==

Barbados bullfinches construct a globular [[Bird nest|nest]], with a side entrance, in a [[tree]] or [[shrub]]. The species lays two to three spotted [[Egg (biology)|eggs]].

== Behavior ==
Barbados bullfinches are extremely innovative<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Reader|first1=Simon M.|last2=Nover|first2=Daniel|last3=Lefebvre|first3=Louis|date=2002-01-01|title=Locale-Specific Sugar Packet Opening by Lesser Antillean Bullfinches in Barbados (Individuos de Loxigilla noctis abren paquetes de azúcar en una área localizada de Barbados)|jstor=4131071|journal=Journal of Field Ornithology|volume=73|issue=1|pages=82–85|doi=10.1648/0273-8570-73.1.82|hdl=1874/12407|s2cid=14665780|hdl-access=free}}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Ducatez|first1=S.|last2=Audet|first2=J. N.|last3=Lefebvre|first3=L.|date=2013-02-14|title=Independent appearance of an innovative feeding behaviour in Antillean bullfinches|journal=Animal Cognition|language=en|volume=16|issue=3|pages=525–529|doi=10.1007/s10071-013-0612-4|pmid=23408337|s2cid=14815365|issn=1435-9448}}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Lefebvre|first1=Louis|last2=Ducatez|first2=Simon|last3=Audet|first3=Jean-Nicolas|date=2016-03-19|title=Feeding innovations in a nested phylogeny of Neotropical passerines|journal=Phil. Trans. R. Soc. B|language=en|volume=371|issue=1690|article-number=20150188|doi=10.1098/rstb.2015.0188|issn=0962-8436|pmc=4780530|pmid=26926278}}</ref> and tame birds. Barbados bullfinches living in urban environments were found to have better [[problem-solving]] skills and a better [[immunocompetence]] than the ones living in rural areas of Barbados.<ref name=":0">{{Cite journal|last1=Audet|first1=Jean-Nicolas|last2=Ducatez|first2=Simon|last3=Lefebvre|first3=Louis|date=2016-03-01|title=The town bird and the country bird: problem solving and immunocompetence vary with urbanization|journal=Behavioral Ecology|language=en|volume=27|issue=2|pages=637–644|doi=10.1093/beheco/arv201|issn=1045-2249|doi-access=free}}</ref> As for their temperament, urban Barbados bullfinches were found to be [[Boldness|bolder]] but more [[Neophobia|neophobic]] than their rural counterparts.<ref name=":0" />
